This is a highly risk-averse portfolio but would still provide a reasonably high income of nearly $69,000 on a capital base of $1 million. Since 2018, on an annualized basis, the growth of this portfolio (including the income) would have been 9.3%, not all that bad, considering 25% of the portfolio is in cash-like securities.

Investments:

We will have 25% of the portfolio invested in totally safe cash-like investments to provide a safety cushion to the portfolio. In addition, nearly 40% (20% each) is invested in two of the very popular, low-cost, and relatively safe dividend and income ETFs, Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and JPMorgan Equity Premium Income (JEPI). These two positions are going to provide not only a high income but also the necessary growth to the broader portfolio.

We also have 35% of the portfolio invested in two income vehicles (BIDZ and CEFS). These two are going to provide high income, diversification, and exposure to the broad economy, but also add to the risk.

VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) is an ETF that invests in a portfolio of BDCs (Business Development Companies). BIZD is an ETF that holds about 30 individual BDCs, so the risk is fairly distributed. BIZD is a passive fund that allocates based on market-cap weighting. Some of the largest BDCs, such as Ares Capital (ARCC) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), are its largest holdings.

Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS) is a "fund of funds" ETF, and as such, it is highly diversified. It invests in closed-end funds and currently holds close to 100 funds. It is managed by SABA Capital Group and is an actively managed fund. It has a rather short history of less than 10 years. It also pays a monthly distribution, which accounts for roughly an 8.4% yield on the current prices. The expense ratio is high with the fund's fees plus the expenses of its underlying fund holdings, but it is a cost for wide diversification and active management. This is pretty much in line with funds of this nature.

Strategy 2: High-Income (8% income) Strategy

This strategy is for folks who believe in earning a high income from their investments, whether they use it all or reinvest it back into the portfolio. Sure, the high income comes with some added risk, but if the portfolio is properly diversified into different asset classes and sectors, and selects the best possible fund in each sector, the risk is very manageable. In the long term, such a portfolio can possibly provide market-matching returns as well.

Obviously, if you are someone who is comfortable with a 3% to 4% income from their portfolios because you either have a large amount of capital or income coming from other sources, then this strategy may not be for you.

The portfolio presented below will aim to generate a minimum of 8% income.

The securities/funds are: (JEPQ), (AMLP), (JFNIX), (O), (ARCC), (MAIN), (PDI), (XFLT), (ADX), (RFI), (UTG), and (PEO).

Table 2: High-Income Portfolio:

The portfolio shown above is highly diversified, spread across many sectors and asset classes. The portfolio has a total of 12 funds (including REITs and BDCs) with equal allocation. The average yield at this time is very close to 9%.

Strategy 3: The Hedging (SWAN - Sleep Well At Night) Investing

We are going to present a ROTATION method for income investors, using FOUR-PLUS-ONE securities. The first four are the core investments, while the fifth one is the hedging security. This strategy can also provide a significant amount of monthly income (estimated at 6%), in addition to providing hedging and potentially market-beating growth. Three of the four securities selected provide high income (close to 8% to 10%), while the fourth one provides no income. Also, at times, one or more of the regular securities will be replaced with the hedging security, the Treasury fund (with much less income). So, on average, we expect the portfolio income to be roughly 6%.

Rotation Method: There are four primary securities, each being allocated 25%. Every month, we will calculate the total performance returns (including any distributions) over the previous three months and seven months for each of the four securities, as well as for the fallback security. We will assign 50% weightage to each of the two periods (3-month and 7-month performance) to calculate the average performance. If the performance of any of the primary securities is lower than the risk-free return provided by Cash (or 1-3 month Treasury), then the primary security is replaced by the Treasury fund (IEF – the mid-term Treasury, in our example) for the next month; otherwise, it is retained. This process is repeated at the end of each month.

The four securities plus the fallback security are:

  • (ADX) Adams Diversified Equity Fund
  • (RQI) Cohen&Steers Quality Income Realty
  • (KYN) Kayne Anderson MLP Investment Closed Fund
  • (QLD) ProShares Ultra QQQ
  • The fallback security: (IEF) 7-10 Year Treasury fund

We admit, this rotational method is a bit complex and may not suit everyone.

Concluding Thoughts

We have had an incredible run so far in 2025, after the collapse back in April. Will the bull run continue for the rest of the year? A lot depends on where the interest rates go from here. The market has baked in at least two rate cuts this year, especially after the last jobs report.

Markets will always remain uncertain. So, it is advisable to invest regularly. Even if you have a lump sum or a large chunk of money to invest, it is better to invest gradually over a period of time.

In this article, we have presented three strategies to serve the needs of a wider audience. What you may choose should be based on your individual needs, preferences, and risk tolerance. One can also combine these three strategies to form a really good SWAN-type portfolio.

data.title: BTCS: I've Changed My Mind (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I initiated coverage of BTCS Inc (NASDAQ:BTCS) for Seeking Alpha a little over a month ago given the company's pivot to a digital asset treasury business. At that time, I rated the stock a 'hold' and specifically mentioned dilution as a concern because of some of the language in the ATM prospectus. Most notably that BTCS wouldn't give specifics regarding how much capital from the ATM would be utilized for Ethereum (ETH-USD) purchases. Since then, we have a Q2 earnings report to assess as well as clearer data regarding ETH acquisitions. In this update, we'll look at that data and I'll explain why I think an upgrade is warranted despite the risks.

Q2 Earnings

For the quarter ended June, BTCS reported $2.8 million in top line revenue - all from Blockchain infrastructure operations. The company separates its blockchain ops revenue into two different buckets; NodeOps and Builder+. NodeOps derives revenue from natively staking assets on-chain as well as through delegation. Builder+ works a bit different as BTCS is purchasing block space and then bundling mempool transactions that it then proposes to validators. This is how the company explains the process for Ethereum in the 10-Q:

Revenue recognition for these activities, conducted through Builder+, entails the recognition of gas fees (or “transaction fees”) and priority fees (or “tips”) earned in exchange for successfully constructing blocks of bundled transactions and having these blocks selected and proposed by a validator to the Ethereum network for validation and successfully finalized on the network.

The breakout between NodeOps and Builder+ for both the quarter ended and six months ended June looks like this:

At 86% of blockchain ops revenue so far this year, Builder+ is by far the biggest driver of BTCS's top line. NodeOps generates income from multiple different smart contract chains including Ethereum, Cosmos (ATOM-USD), Solana (SOL-USD), NEAR Protocol (NEAR-USD) and Axie Infinity (AXS-USD). Builder+ generated revenue primarily from ETH with $3.5 million of the $3.9 million Builder+ revenue this year coming from Ethereum; the remainder came from BNB Chain (BNB-USD).

While Builder+ drives top-line, the margins for these businesses are quite different according to the 10-Q:

Revenue H1 2025 H1 2024
NodeOps $602,263 $903,692
Builder+ $3,858,870 $108,886
Cost of Rev H1 2025 H1 2024
NodeOps $60,021 $99,367
Builder+ $4,361,771 $230,106
Margin H1 2025 H1 2024
NodeOps 90.03% 89.00%
Builder+ -13.03% -111.33%

Source: BTCS, 10-Q

The margins for NodeOps are fantastic. Margins for Builder+, while much better in 2025, indicate that the segment actually loses money. The company says that the cost of revenues for Builder+ includes node maintenance employee salaries as well as infrastructure hosting. In any case, it looks as though BTCS Inc would be better off doing nothing at all and sitting on its ETH rather than consolidating transactions - at least in a low-fee environment.

As I understand the model, the company purchases block space through 'a fixed non-negotiable' fee paid to network validators. This could theoretically make transaction bundling profitable for BTCS in a higher-fee environment. But we'll have to see how that plays out should Ethereum ever return to a fee-growth network.

ETH Treasury and AAVE Strategy

Subsequent to earnings, BTCS has disclosed owning over 70k ETH. At current Ethereum prices, the company's ETH holdings have a market value of just under $300 million:

Yet, the market cap of just $208.5 million seemingly presents an arbitrage. With most of the other 'ETH Treasury' companies in the equity market trading at premiums to NAV, BTCS trades at a 30% discount to ETH holdings. Screaming buy? It's a bit more complicated than that.

Subsequent to the company's earnings report and ETH purchases, BTCS borrowed an additional $47 million from lending protocol AAVE (AAVE-USD):

As of August 12, 2025, the Company had approximately $51,702,000 in outstanding borrowings, inclusive of accrued interest, collateralized by approximately 38,400 ETH with a fair market value of approximately $176,062,000, based on the ETH closing price of $4,584 on that date.

It would be a straightforward arbitrage to buy BTCS at a 30% discount to holdings if the ETH was natively staked and earning passive yield. However, I think the discount for BTCS is likely due to concerns about the company's AAVE borrowings even though BTCS's AAVE loan is over-collateralized. Admittedly, AAVE yields have moved against BTCS since it took out its latest loans:

At present, borrowing Tether (USDT-USD) through AAVE has an APY of 6.5% percent. During the time period that BTCS took out the loans, AAVE USDT APYs were closer to 5.5%. Meaning, BTCS has already seen its borrowing cost increase in less than two weeks. The reason for this spike in borrowing cost is because the amount of USDT available to borrow through AAVE has fallen from $1.4 billion on August 1st to just $442 million as of article submission. But as yields from borrowing increase, so too does the yield from providing liquidity. Thus, I expect AAVE's USDT borrow rates may actually come back down as more liquidity chases dollar-denominated yield that exceed the rate of t-bills.

Even though BTCS is staking the ETH to generate yield, staking rewards from the Ethereum blockchain have fallen below 3%. So on the surface, the company is borrowing at 6.5% to stake (or lend, if that helps) at 3% while the value of the asset being staked may have peaked temporarily. But even still, I think the discount for BTCS shares is too extreme given what the company has actually been doing.

The table below is my rough estimate at what the company would generate in earnings given current borrow cost, supply cost, and staking yield:

ETH Strategy ETH $ Value Rate Annual
Pledged ETH 38,400 $163,776,000 2.30% $3,766,848
Purchased with Borrowed USDT 16,100 $68,666,500 2.92% $2,005,062
Borrowed USDT -$52,000,000 6.50% -$3,380,000
Net Earnings From ETH Strategy $2,391,910

Source: BTCS, Analyst's estimates at $4,265 ETH price

Even at sub-3% staking yields, 6.5% USDT borrow cost, and 2.3% from collaraterlized ETH, this strategy is well in the black by my math. In fact, even if ETH fell to $3,500 and USDT borrow costs through AAVE rose to 9%, this strategy would still be slightly profitable.

So what is BTCS worth?

I think the right way to look at BTCS valuation is 'net ETH' rather than the market cap to Treasury holdings metrics that we generally see with these kinds of companies.

BTCS 'net ETH' Calculation
Total ETH 70,140
Loan $ Value $52,000,000
Loan in ETH 12,381
Net ETH 57,759
$ Value at $4,200 $242,588,000
Shares outstanding 48,000,000
Net ETH Per Share $5.05
True Discount 14.72%

Source: BTCS Inc, Analyst's estimates

The math in the table above shows a hypothetical situation in which BTCS sells $52 million in ETH to pay off its AAVE loan. This would require 12,381 ETH at current prices, leaving almost 58k ETH and a gain of nearly 4k ETH on the loan. It also indicates that at an ETH price of $4,200 per coin, BTCS has a 'net ETH' value slightly above $5 per share.

Key Risks

As I mentioned in my first piece covering BTCS shares, dilution remains a major concern for BTCS shareholders. We've already seen shares outstanding explode since the end of Q2:

Furthermore, the company had a small fraction of the ETH it currently has when I last covered the company in July yet the share price is essentially flat.

Something else to consider is Short Interest in this stock is getting aggressive with nearly 14% of shares outstanding currently sold short. This is a larger level of short interest currently than what we see in other ETH Treasury companies like SharpLink Gaming (SBET) or Bitmine Immersion (BMNR). From where I sit, it's hard to blame the bears for this level of conviction given BTCS's history of operating losses.

Closing Thoughts

This may come as a surprise to some of my readers, but I'm actually much less pessimistic about BTCS at this point in time. That change in sentiment is mainly due to the equity valuation relative to the ETH held in reserve. Sure, some of this has been pledged as collateral. But given the company's collateralization ratio, ETH would likely have to fall below $2,000 per coin before any loan liquidation fears would manifest. I don't think that's a concern short of an economic calamity akin to COVID.

If we observe the company's valuation through the net ETH metric, BTCS shares are trading at a discount and should likely be closer to $5. I've changed my mind on BTCS. I actually think it's an interesting ETH Treasury spec on its AAVE strategy alone. Factor in that it's trading at a discount to net ETH value, and I think it's one of the better ways to play ETH treasuries in the market right now. To be sure, the negative margins from Builder+ are a concern. But those losses are more than made up for by the spread from the company's AAVE protocol usage. Short Interest is high and growing. I don't mind being a contrarian here. In my view, BTCS is a 'buy.'
